data.title: PepsiCo's Rebound Seems Short-Lived: I Would Sell The Pop Of A Fragile Comeback
data.description:
Introduction

PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) (NEOE:PEP:CA) is a new entry among the tickers I cover. The company needs no introduction since we all know its iconic beverage and many of us probably appreciate it. Whether it tastes better than a Coke or not, let us leave it open for debate.

In our case, we simply need to know that the company extends its reach outside of beverage, also running a food business, which is actually the larger of the two, with sales split 58%-42% in food's favor. PepsiCo earns 56% of its revenues in the U.S. and the remaining 46% internationally. When we look at the main products, we see that PepsiCo Beverages makes up around 30% of its sales, followed by Frito-Lay North America. But when we look at the operating profit, Frito-Lay's contribution stands out, accounting for 39% of total operating profits. Recently, Lay's has been struggling, and a big part of PepsiCo's domestic turnaround depends on the relaunch of this brand.

Overall, PepsiCo reported $92B in FY2024 sales (flat YoY), with a core operating profit of $14.7B and a FCF of $7.5B. Let's immediately talk about this: PepsiCo is a mature company that can be well considered a cash cow. Its ability to generate billions in FCF makes it stand out as a fantastic dividend payer that income-seeking investors usually own. Last year, the company paid $7.2B in dividends and added another $1B via share repurchases. This is a 4.4% yield based on today's market cap. Currently, the fwd dividend yield is 4.2%, which surely makes many investors flock to this stock as its share price has compressed from $200 to $125. As a matter of fact, the share price slipped in the past two years because of soft revenue growth, volume pressure due to consumer pullback on discretionary spending for snacks and drinks, and fears of a more healthy behavior by consumers that could hit PepsiCo's business.

Well, today the stock reported earnings, and investors are celebrating, pushing PepsiCo's share price up by 7%. Let's then see why.

PepsiCo's Q2 Earnings Review

PepsiCo posted a top and bottom line beat, with revenue up 2.1% YoY (consensus of +1.2% YoY) to $22.73B and EPS of $2.12 vs. the forecast of $2.03. Investors' enthusiasm sparked when PepsiCo reaffirmed its guidance targeting a low-single-digit revenue increase for this year. It was considered a sign of confidence after quarters of sluggish and weak sales.

What drove PepsiCo's results? Here, the picture is a bit mixed. PepsiCo Foods North America grew 0.7% YoY, while Beverages North America declined by 0.2%. Together, they accounted for $13.28B. However, the international beverage business did well, with a 3.2% YoY growth. EMEA food grew 8.4%, and Asia Pacific grew 0.5%. However, LatAm Foods declined by 7.3%. Since EMEA is the largest of the four, it more than compensated for LatAm's decline.

One caveat, if you go through the main metrics, you will see that PepsiCo's quarterly operating profit declined from $4B to $1.8B in one year. This is because PepsiCo took a non-cash write-down of $1.86B on the value of two brands it owns (Rockstar Energy and Be & Cherry), booking an impairment charge.

However, when we consider PepsiCo's sales, while we see that revenues overall increased by 1% in Q2 and 0.3% YtD, we see that the cost of sales grew 3.9% in Q2, leading to a lower gross profit margin.

During the earnings call, cost management and operational flexibility were one of the key topics that were discussed. PepsiCo did shut down two plants to reduce excess manufacturing capacity while still having manufacturing room to grow. At the same time, PepsiCo's CFO announced the company will have 70% more productivity in the second half of the year compared to the first half. This means PepsiCo has concentrated its manufacturing activity in fewer plants that will work more efficiently. This should help the company's fixed cost structure and its impact on the company's earnings.

As a result, PepsiCo announced its guidance can reasonably target the low end of the company's long-term top-line algorithm through the end of the year. Yet, its management could not help but explain that affordability and health are two key drivers that will determine PepsiCo's sales. This means we could probably see a reacceleration of growth at the expense of a little bit of profitability.

Now, when we look at PepsiCo's financial data, I read numbers that immediately temper any renewed enthusiasm.

First of all, a $1.86B impairment is simply ugly. Secondly, the company's total debt keeps rising and is now above $48.5B. The company's net debt is $40B, which means its net debt/EBITDA ratio is 2.3x. Although its leverage ratio is not extremely high, PepsiCo is indeed quite leveraged and is not using the cash it generates to stabilize its balance sheet. Moreover, due to high-interest rates and the issuance of new debt, PepsiCo pays over $1.5B in interest expense per year. It seems the company prioritizes acquisitions, dividends, and share repurchases.

Right now, the dividend yielding 4.2% is surely attractive. But is it sustainable? The payout ratio is already above 67%, and this leaves little room to keep it growing at 7% per year, as we see PepsiCo did in the past 5 years. While I believe PepsiCo will do anything it can before cutting its dividend, I think it is reasonable to expect much slower dividend growth going forward.

PepsiCo's valuation also seems attractive. After all, the stock has never traded at an 18 PE for the past decade. However, what truly stands out as concerning is the fwd PEG ratio of 5. This is extremely expensive and helps us visualize what we are losing when we invest in PepsiCo: there is simply no growth. If this stagnant situation continues, the stock is at risk of being commoditized and treated as such. This would further compress the earnings multiple to around a 12x, and the PEG ratio would skyrocket.

Using my DCF, I assumed a 2.5% FCF growth for the next 5 years, which already seems a bit generous. Plugging in Pepsi's other financials, the result is that the stock is now trading above its fair value, which I consider to be around $125. Right now, I think it is hard to think that PepsiCo will truly rebound. The horizon is cloudy, and I see no real catalyst that can help change the narrative around PepsiCo. Therefore, I rate the stock as a sell and suggest taking advantage of this pop to exit the position.

data.title: Synovus: Growth Initiatives Are Accelerating Results
data.description:
Shares of Synovus Financial (NYSE:SNV) have recovered strongly from their April lows, nearing a 52-week high, and are now up solidly from a year ago. After the bell on Wednesday, the company reported solid results, speaking to improvement in its loan book over the past two years. This sent shares up 2% in early trading Thursday. I last covered Synovus in April, upgrading shares to a “buy,” and since then, SNV has returned 30%, far outpacing the market’s 20% recovery. With updated financials and shares having passed my $48 price target, now is a good time to revisit Synovus.

In the company’s second quarter, Synovus earned $1.48, blowing past estimates by $0.22. Across the board, the company reported strong results with its credit performance particularly impressive. While deposit growth has been sluggish, the bank’s expansion efforts into middle market lending are gaining traction. Synovus also maintains a solid balance sheet, enabling it to take advantage of the market drop and repurchase $21 million of stock during the quarter.

If there was one negative, it was that Synovus did see some deposit attrition during the quarter. It has $49.9 billion of deposits, down 1% from last year, though core deposits were up 1%. There was a $400 million drop in public funds' deposits, and this has been an area where the company has been shifting focus, given less attractive economics. It also allowed some brokered deposits to roll off its balance sheet as these are the most expensive sources of funding. One positive is that Synovus now has $11.7 billion of noninterest-bearing deposits, up slightly from Q1 and flat from last year.

Synovus has a balance sheet with ample liquidity, which has enabled it to let high-cost funding leave, and core deposits are broadly stable. Over coming quarters, I would like to see core deposit growth accelerate a bit given plans for ongoing loan growth, but I am broadly comfortable with the bank’s deposit experience. In Q2, SNV had a 2.22% cost of deposit, down 4bps from Q1, given some of the favorable mix items I have discussed. SNV has been able to pass on about half of the Fed rate cuts to its depositors, a strong performance.

On the other side of its balance sheet, we are seeing ongoing momentum in Synovus’s lending efforts, which has been particularly impressive given the muted loan growth most banks are seeing. Loans grew by $888 million sequentially to $43.5 billion. This was up a modest 1% from last year. As you can see below, essentially all of the company’s loan growth was focused in business lending (“C&I”), consistent with its efforts to expand middle-market lending while reducing the focus on commercial real estate.

In addition to enabling accelerating loan growth, this pivot is also helping to improve credit quality with credit metrics steadily improving. During the quarter, Synovus had just 17bps of charge-offs. This is down substantially from last year, though I still expect charge-offs to be at least 20bps this year, given some economic downside risks. Alongside this improvement in charge-offs, we have seen nonperforming loans improve. SNV now has 59bps of NPLs, down 8bps sequentially. Because of this improvement, it took just $3 million in provisions from $11 million last quarter. Lower credit loss provisions drove much of its earnings beat and exceeded my expectations.

Synovus has $514 million in reserves, or 1.18% of loans. This provides 200% coverage of NPLs. Given the company’s ongoing credit improvement, I view this level of reserves as healthy and do not see the need to build reserves, outside of supporting ongoing loan growth. Importantly, it has just $1.7 billion in office exposure. This pool has a low 55.5% loan to value, speaking to the bank’s conservative underwriting standards, which should keep losses minimal.

Elsewhere, the company has a $10.6 billion securities portfolio. There is currently a $700 million unrealized loss on this portfolio as, like most banks, Synovus purchased bonds when rates were lower. As bonds mature, we should see this unrealized loss gradually diminish and the portfolio yield increase. Indeed, its securities yield is up 45 bps from last year to 3.49%.

Thanks to fixed reinvestment and improving deposit costs, we are seeing the company better monetize is asset base. Synovus had a 3.37% net interest margin (“NIM”), up 2bps sequentially and 15bps from last year. As a result of wider margins, net interest income of $460 million was up 1% sequentially and 6% from last year. I expect to see ongoing NIM and NII growth, given loan growth and fixed reinvestment.

Synovus also remains well capitalized with a 10.9% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio, leaving it well-positioned to finance ongoing growth. Given its capital position, I view its 2.9% dividend yield as very secure. I expect buyback activity to be modest with SNV likely to retain capital to finance loan growth. Management wants to maintain capital at or near current levels. Eventually as its unrealized loss (which is excluded from capital calculations) declines below $500 million, I can see SNV operating closer to a 10-10.5% CET1 range, though that is more likely a late-2026 occurrence.

Alongside results, Synovus raised guidance. Most notably, it now sees revenue growth of 5-7% from 3-6% previously. This speaks to its high conviction in accelerating loan growth as well as the benefits of just 2 rate cuts (which matches my own expectations). While I have generally been cautious on loan growth as tariff uncertainty may cause businesses to delay some capital spending decisions, SNV drove significant sequential growth in a particularly difficult quarter. That positions it very well to keep growing loans into the second half of the year in my view. Accordingly, I view guidance as broadly achievable.

I saw Synovus generate earnings of $5 when I last covered shares in April. At that time, I saw some risk of further credit reserve builds if tariffs created a recession; that no longer appears likely. At the same time, I am now more optimistic on the bank’s ability to grow its loan book given recent initiatives. Accordingly, I am raising my earnings outlook to $5.20-$5.50, even assuming a slight uptick in charge-offs relative to Q2. That leaves shares right around 10x earnings. I generally view 9-12x as the appropriate multiple range for regional banks, depending on their capital position and growth prospects. With nearly an 11% CET1 and accelerating loan growth, I believe SNV should be positioned in the upper tier of the sector.

At an 11x multiple, shares should trade to the $58-$60 range in my view. That gives shares about 9% upside and a nearly 12% total return prospect including its dividend. While I expect returns to moderate from its strong 30% gain since my last recommendation, this still is an attractive return that passes my 10% hurdle for a buy rating. Synovus’s initiatives are gaining momentum, boosting growth, and improving credit quality. Even after their rally, I recommend buying shares in this regional bank.

data.title: B. Riley Financial: Near-Term Bankruptcy Filing Increasingly Unlikely (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Note

I have covered B. Riley Financial, Inc. or "B. Riley" (NASDAQ:RILY) (NASDAQ:RILYL) (NASDAQ:RILYP) (NASDAQ:RILYK) (NASDAQ:RILYN) (NASDAQ:RILYG) (NASDAQ:RILYT) (NASDAQ:RILYZ) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

It's been four months since my last update on beleaguered investment bank and financial services provider B. Riley Financial.

Since that time, the company has taken additional steps to address near-term debt maturities, appointed a new CFO and was granted additional time to regain compliance with Nasdaq listing requirements.

In aggregate, B. Riley has exchanged approximately $355 million in existing unsecured notes for $229 million in new second lien notes:

As a result, the 2026 debt wall has decreased from approximately $723 million to between $443 million and $465 million, depending on the unspecified $22 million notes exchange in April.

Assuming the April notes exchange included only the company's January 2028 unsecured notes (RILYT), total debt would look as follows:

Please note that the company has almost exhausted its $250 million second lien note capacity now.

In addition, to the above-discussed debt exchanges, B. Riley sold its advisory services business ("GlassRatner") to funds managed by TorQuest Partners for $117.8 million. The divestment will result in an approximately $66 million gain.

Moreover, the company also gained additional financial flexibility by amending the terms of its $160 million senior secured term loan facility with funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management, L.P. ("Oaktree"):

The amended Term Loan Facility provides incremental flexibility, including: a new investment basket that enables an incremental $100m to facilitate transactions using B. Riley Financial's balance sheet; a $30 million investment basket for parent company investments upsized from $20 million; and the ability to deploy up to $25 million of cash to reduce other indebtedness, including through the potential repurchase of the Company's unsecured notes.

While the company hasn't updated investors on its financial results and balance sheet since early March, the sale of GlassRatner should have lifted the company's pro forma cash balance above $300 million:

With the 2026 debt wall looking more manageable and persistent senior lender support, the company's securities have rallied across the board in recent weeks with B. Riley's preferred (RILYL, RILYP) and common shares (NASDAQ:RILY) leading the pack:

However, the company's unsecured 2028 notes (RILYT, RILYZ) are still trading at a more than 50% discount to face value as market participants remain wary of close to $1.0 billion in 2028 debt maturities.

While B. Riley has done a decent job addressing near-term debt maturities, gaining financial flexibility, and raising additional funds, future profitability and cash flow will be pressured by the required sale of premium assets like Great American, GlassRatner and the company's brand portfolio, just to name a few.

However, with a near-term bankruptcy filing increasingly unlikely, I am raising my rating on the company's common and preferred shares from "Sell" to "Hold".

Bottom Line

B. Riley Financial continues to make progress in its efforts to clean up the financial mess caused by the ill-advised decision to become involved with the 2023 management buyout of Franchise Group.

Recent debt exchange transactions represent an important step towards addressing near-term debt maturities, but with the company's second lien debt capacity almost exhausted, B. Riley will have to figure out other ways to deal with the remaining 2026 unsecured notes.

In addition, the required sale of premium assets will impact the company's profitability and cash generation going forward.

That said, with a near-term bankruptcy filing increasingly unlikely, I am raising my rating on the company's common and preferred shares from "Sell" to "Hold".

data.title: Tariffs, Schmarriffs
data.description:
Markets made new highs Thursday, further embarrassing the recession forecasters still yelling “Fire!” in a theater now selling standing-room tickets. Evidently, Wall Street misplaced the memo proclaiming the end was nigh.

Level Change 7/17/25 (%)

+0.5 Dow

+0.8 Nasdaq

+0.8 Nasdaq 100

+0.5 S&P 500

+1.1 S&P 400

+1.1 S&P 600

It’s been a bruising stretch for bears.

On April 8, with stocks scraping their lows and tariff fear at fever pitch, they stood tall and solemn, predicting an imminent recession. Since then, the S&P has rallied 26%, making fools of forecasters and turning index investors into reluctant geniuses. Undeterred, bears have recycled their prophecy for higher prices - same warning, different altitude.

And yet, the climb continues.

Thursday gave bulls a refill, thanks to American consumers indulging their national pastime: spending like tomorrow’s paycheck is guaranteed. June retail sales beat across the board, and the “control group” - the Fed’s favorite GDP proxy - posted its best gain since March. Nobody told the mall there’s a slowdown.

Initial jobless claims fell for a fifth straight week, down to 221K. That’s the lowest since April, suggesting that, despite doomsday tariff talk, employers are still hoarding workers like toilet paper in 2020. Continuing claims even slipped below consensus. So much for cracks in the labor market.

No shift in the rate cut story, though market pricing tilted a touch more hawkish, now projecting 41 basis points of easing by year-end. The Fed has been assigned the role of monetary Hamlet: to cut or not to cut. With strong economic data and political pressure competing for center stage, Powell may regret not spending July in Iceland.

Speaking of pressure, President Trump offered mildly constructive remarks on trade talks with India and the EU. This saga drags on, though the script is exhausted and the cast appears to be checking their watches. Europe, reportedly working up tariffs on US services, seems ready for a second act, but this diplomacy-as-drama looks one rewrite from cancellation.

With the supposedly bad news now more than three months baked in, stocks rose. Nvidia (NVDA +1.0%) led the charge, a reminder that when in doubt, buy the GPU overlord.

So ended another session where nothing changed and stocks rallied. Bears insist it’s all built on sand. But as of Thursday, it’s a record-breaking, profit-spitting, job-holding, tariff-dodging sandcastle - and it’s holding just fine.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CDW Corporation: Earnings Growth Needs To Return
data.description:
Company Description

CDW Corporation (NASDAQ:CDW) is a leading provider of integrated information technology solutions, with the majority of its revenue coming from within the United States. The company has four reportable segments: Corporate, Public, Small Business, and Other (CDW UK & CDW Canada). CDW has a market cap of almost $25B and employs more than 15,000 people. The stock has an excellent total return over the past decade of more than 475%, resulting in a compound annual growth rate of slightly above 19%.

Quality Financial Metrics

As always, we're going to examine some key financial metrics to assess how the company has performed.

For the majority of the past decade the revenue per share grew at a very healthy rate, with a clear slowdown over the past two fiscal years. From 2015 through 2022 the RPS grew at 12.65% per year, with the largest increase occurring from 2021 to 2022 at 16.8%. Recently, the RPS has retreated, with declines of nearly 10% in FY23 and 1.2% in FY24.

The gross profit margin was fairly steady for the first seven years of the chart, but over the past three it has expanded from 17.1% in 2021 to nearly 22% in 2024. This expansion has occurred as a result of a strategic focus on more profitable business lines, including: Cloud and SaaS solutions, along with cybersecurity and IT workflow optimization.

The return on invested capital metric began the past decade at a sub-10% rate before rising to a peak of 17.1% in fiscal year 2020. Although the ROIC has declined since that peak, it has still hovered around the mid-teens. Ideally in the near future we'll see this metric climb closer to my usual threshold of 20%.

Dividend Data

CDW currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.625 per share, resulting in an annual payout of $2.50, and a forward dividend yield of 1.42%. The company's 10-year dividend growth rate is a robust 26%; however, it has clearly slow over the years as indicated by the blue line. I believe the declining revenue over the past two years has led to these weaker than normal dividend hikes. On a positive note, the payout ratio is still very low, so, although the dividend growth has slowed, I don't think it will stop altogether anytime soon.

Recent Earnings

In early May, CDW announced their Q1 2025 earnings with a double-beat. Non-GAAP EPS was $2.15 per share topping the consensus by $0.19, on revenue of $5.2B which also exceeded analyst expectations by $260M and was a 6.8% increase from the same quarter a year ago.

Breaking the earnings down by segment...the Corporate segment generated 43% of the overall sales for the company which was $2.24B, a 4.7% year-over-year increase. This increase was driven by sales of notebooks and mobile devices, partially offset by a decline in data storage and servers.

The Small Business piece of the business saw sales rise by $24M to $404.6M for the quarter, a solid 6.2% increase compared to Q1 2024. These results were due to increases in sales across all product lines. Conversely, operating income decreased $3M, or almost 7%, due to lower gross profit for this segment.

There are three divisions within the Public segment, with Healthcare seeing the biggest sales increase at more than 17% to $687.9M for the quarter. The Education division also saw improved sales of 9% to $652.4M, from $596.8M. While the Government division incurred a small decline in revenue of about $6M, or 1%, to $537.8M. Overall, Public segment revenue increased 9%, driven by increases in notebooks & mobile devices in Education and Healthcare, coupled with an increase in software within the Healthcare division; however, these increases were partially offset by a decrease in other hardware.

And finally, revenue in the Other segment rose $49M, or almost 8%, resulting from a rise in sales of notebooks & mobile devices in the UK.

The market was very encouraged by the earnings release with the stock rising 7% on the day, and another 5% in the week after the release.

The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The final consideration is to determine if the stock is trading for a discount or premium at its current levels and to do so we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

My custom valuation model indicates a slight overvaluation right now, with the stock trading for about $176 per share, and a fair price closer to $166. Following a 20% increase in the FCF/share in 2023, the stock followed suit increasing close to 30%. In 2024, CDW lost most of its 2023 gains, with the stock declining more than 22%, and thus far in 2025, it is up less than 1%.

The long-term estimated rate of return for CDW is a modest 6.06%, with the components of that projection being a 1.42% forward dividend yield, a return to fair value factor of -1.17%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 5.81%.

Final Thoughts

CDW has performed very well for the majority of the past decade, but has struggled in recent years. The revenue has slipped recently, but the company still generates a healthy gross profit, which is expanding, and a respectable return on invested capital. The dividend growth was very impressive initially but has obviously slowed, hopefully we'll see more promising dividend increases return in the near future. The most recent earnings report was very positive with the stock surging significantly following the release; however, this led to the stock becoming overvalued, albeit only slightly. Couple the overvaluation with a weak long-term projected rate of return that comes up short of the typical threshold of at least 10%, and I must rate the stock a Sell. Until we see forecasted earnings growth pick back up in a significant way, or the stock becomes significantly undervalued an investment right now doesn't make sense to me.

data.title: AGNC Investment Q2 Earnings Preview: Favoring Yield Capture On Commons Over Buying Preferreds
data.description:
AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC) is set to release its second quarter earnings results on July 21st, which could mark a substantial occasion given the mREIT's yield profile and the array of inflection points that have emerged in credit markets. Moreover, we've identified AGNC as an actionable yield capture idea and believe that its Q2 earnings report will provide substance to our core thesis.

Herewith is an analysis of our base case, including an outlook towards AGNC's Q2 earnings, a view of the mREIT's capital structure, and a revision of key value drivers.

A Quick Description of AGNC Investment Corp.

To those who are unaware, AGNC Investment Corp. is a mortgage REIT. The company relies on borrowing from short-term lenders and invests the proceeds in longer-term assets.

Though minor diversifiers exist, the fund's asset-to-liability mix of choice is long-term agency mortgages and repo facilities, providing an opportunity to achieve excess spread. Though simple in theory, rates aren't stable, requiring the vehicle to hedge or adjust its asset-to-liability structure to mitigate risks.

Herewith is a visual extracted from AGNC's Q1 presentation, conveying the mREIT's common share returns since inception, a description of its objectives, and a decomposition of its capital structure.

Earnings

Q2 Expectations

AGNC is anticipated to release its earnings on July 21st, followed by a presentation on July 22nd. Seeking Alpha's sampling shows that analysts anticipate AGNC to deliver GAAP-adjusted earnings per share of 20 cents, up from the 2 cents reported via AGNC's Q1 results. Moreover, normalized earnings per share is expected to settle at 41 cents, down from the 44 cents achieved in Q1. As for revisions, AGNC has received eight upward revisions in the last 90 days, which is an encouraging sign, seeing as the mREIT has missed three of its last four quarterly EPS targets.

The following visual was extracted from AGNC's Q1 investor presentation to illustrate a time series of AGNC's quarterly results. The inconsistency of past returns underscores the relevance of AGNC's Q2 report. For example, the mREIT reported severe fluctuations in comprehensive income to common shareholders in recent quarters, suggesting cyclical and seasonal performance.

Our View

Rate Dynamics – Asset Level

Rate dynamics, including mortgage rates, treasury yields, and repos are exceptionally important to consider in AGNC's case given that about 74.4% of its balance sheet comprises rate-sensitive fair value agency securities. Moreover, AGNC has a duration gap, which settled at 0.4 in Q1 and 0.3 in Q4, affirming the need to dial in on rate movements from a valuation standpoint.

Next up is a view of AGNC's agency portfolio, once more extracted from the firm's Q1 presentation. The data points to AGNC's overwhelming exposure to 30-year fixed rate terms. Approximately 90% of the book comprises 30-year fixed-rate specified pools with a weighted average loan age of 35 (around 3-years) and forecasted conditional prepayment rates of 8%. In essence, AGNC locked-in most of its pool at high mortgage rates (going by WALA) with long durations. The fund's shorter duration mortgages have substantially higher WALAs, suggesting AGNC might've bought them in the secondary market at discounts to maintain high portfolio yields.

Here's what we think about the agency portfolio's possible performance during Q2.

  • Mortgage rates exceeded the 600 basis point level in September 2022, remaining above those levels ever since. Considering the WALA, we can probably commit to high asset yields being reported in AGNC's Q2 report.
  • 30-year fixed rates have retreated to around 672 basis points after opening the year at 691 basis points. Despite the reversion, mortgage rates are well beyond the 10-year average, allowing AGNC to lock-in new on-the-run mortgages to sustain its revenue guidance.
  • U.S. 10-year yield has ticked up slightly since AGNC last reported, increasing to around 443 basis points on July 14th from 432 basis points on April 24th (shortly AGNC last reported), reflecting a modestly higher base discount rate.
  • Fighting the previous point is the fact that mortgage spreads (30-year mortgage rate - 10-year yield) are at circa 237 basis points (July 10th), down from the 249 basis points recorded on April 24th, communicating minor overall discount rate compression and higher asset valuation potentials.
  • AGNC's Agency Conditional Prepayment Rates were weighted averaged to 8% in Q1. Fed target rates have remained unchanged, but mortgage rates have declined slightly. We think CPR might be revised upward by a few basis points in Q2, knocking a few increments off AGNC's implied earnings.

As per its latest investor presentation: AGNC's credit portfolio settled at $843 million in face value in Q1, paling in comparison to the mREIT's $77.9 billion agency portfolio. Nonetheless, inferences can be made. For example, CMBS exposure of 24% can play an incremental role during a period where ex-farmland commercial delinquencies rose to 1.59% in Q1 from 1.22% a year earlier.

Rate Dynamics – Liability-Level and Hedging

AGNC Investment Corp.'s reported funding and leverage ratio reached 7.5x in Q1, up from 7.2x in Q4. As illustrated in the figure below, repo borrowing is executed via pledging collateral. The rate settled around 4.47% in Q1 but was brought down by interest rate swaps; a discussion follows the diagram.

Herewith are a few things to consider regarding financing.

  • Repo rates remained stuck in Q2. We anticipate lower short-term yields to occur in late 2025, early-2026, possibly resulting in lower short-term rates, especially if funding risk remains within range. That being said, we highly doubt that repo financing cost drifted lower in AGNC's Q2.
  • Swaps can be used to lower financing costs, and the stable MOVE index suggests financing-level swap execution and asset-liability management might've been made easier in Q2. That being said, adjusting financing via swaps can influencing asset level returns due to rate correlations.

  • Hedging with treasuries is a smart and nimble move due to the asset class' liquidity. However, treasury yields shifted notably during stages of Q2, meaning basis risk might've been exposed.

AGNC's Capital Structure

Common Shares

I might be wrong, but I think most investors are interested in AGNC's 15%+ yield instead of its interim price activity, which naturally raises the question: Is the AGNC's yield sustainable? In our view, AGNC's economic returns and profit & loss results are too inconsistent to deliver consistent yield, unless the mREIT returns capital. That said, we think interim spreads are sustainable, and therefore see AGNC's common shares as a good dividend capture or trading opportunity.

Herewith is a compact view of AGNC's recent economic returns, dividends, tangible book value, and comprehensive Profit & Loss.

We don't see AGNC as a buy-to-hold addition. Sure, if acquired at a deep discount at an impressive yield-on-cost, the discussion changes. However, we are looking at an asset trading at a trailing price-to-tangible book of around 1.09x (author's calculations). Moreover, while we see the vehicle's spread holding for the next year or two, we think AGNC will eventually have to recycle into lower-yielding mortgages when mortgage rates drop, adding pressure to the P&L, unless financing costs drop substantially.

The gist of it: AGNC possibly sustains its yield for the next 12 months and receives a small price kicker (if and when short-term rates compress). After that, we may have to reassess.

Preferred

AGNC's preferred shares issuances provide differentiated high-yielding opportunities. AGNC's preferred shares mostly trade at premiums, suggesting investors have rushed into their yields. That said, yields remain pent up relative to benchmarks, illustrated by using the Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX), which currently yields modestly at around 6.10%, meaning it won't make sense disregarding AGNC Investment Corp.'s preferreds without having a closer look.

Let's review the preferred shares' characteristics; a discussion follows.

Key Notes:

  • Shares including, AGNC Investment Corp. CUM 1/1000 7% C (NASDAQ:AGNCN), AGNC Investment Corp. 6.875 DEP REP D (NASDAQ:AGNCM), AGNC Investment Corp. 6.5% DP SH PFD E (NASDAQ:AGNCO), and AGNC Investment Corp. 6.12 DP SH PFD F (NADSAQ:AGNCP), are all fixed-to-floating. AGNC Investment Corp. 7.75% DP PFD G (NASDAQ:AGNCL) remains the only issuance yet to reset.
  • Series G will use the 5-year U.S. treasury rate as a benchmark, the other issuances use 3-month LIBOR.
Series Yield Price Liquidation Spread Call/Reset
C 9.61% $25.35 $25 5.111% 10/15/22'
D 8.96% $24.97 $25 4.332% 04/14/24'
E 9.48% $25.36 $25 4.993% 10/15/22'
F 9.29% $25.08 $25 4.697% 04/15/25'
G 7.83% $24.74 $25 4.39% 11/15/27'

Source: Preferred Stock Channel, SEC.GOV

AGNC Investment Corp.'s preferred shares look respectable. However, we could see prices diminish if U.S. interest rates settle lower due to the adjustable features and premiums (or thin discounts). Moreover, U.S. corporate credit spreads remain complacent, an expansion could devalue AGNC's preferred shares.

The common shares still yield a premium of circa 600+ basis points over various preferred share issuances. While net income attributable to common shareholders spread thinly in Q1, we anticipate a positive spread to sustain for the next few quarters, enabling an opportunity to capture yields on AGNC's ordinary shares.

Risk Factor Consolidation

Firstly, I'd like to reiterated that this is a highly leveraged business. Whether pertaining to its preferred shares, ordinary shares, or repo borrowings, counter-party risk needs to be assessed carefully. If credit spreads blow or bond yields soar, we'd likely see instability occur throughout the capital structure.

In addition to the above, managing asset-liability duration and interest rate sensitivity can be difficult. Herewith is a chart that illustrates a simulation, quantifying how destructive a rate or spread shock can be.

Lastly, AGNC's recent earnings track record has disappointed many. Another earnings miss might results in a sell-off of the common shares, which have already shed over 10% in value over the past year.

Conclusion

Our analysis shows that AGNC Investment Corp.'s second quarter earnings report could reflect sustained asset-level performance, mainly due to the mREIT locking in a substantial amount of long-dated mortgages during a period where rates have been near or above 6%. Repo rates, mortgages spreads, and the 10-year yield remain within a similar range as in Q1, suggesting stable discount rates. Additionally, the MOVE index has been docile, making hedging easier.

A short-term yield capture opportunity may be available via AGNC Investment Corp.'s common share issuance, which currently yields above 15%. The common shares have a positive yield spread of around 600+ basis points versus the various preferred issuances. Though the preferred shares present cumulative features and yield impressively, they have reached their liquidation values, are susceptible to resets, and probably don't hold as much gravitas as AGNC's common shares, unless sustained net interest losses or asset-level valuation concerns appear.

Consensus: Very Bullish AGNC for 6-12 months, Neutral or Soft Buy outlook on Preferred Series C (AGNCN), E (AGNCO), and F (AGNCP).

data.title: Higher Long-Term Interest Rates If Trump Fires Powell From The Fed
data.description:
President Trump has been unhappy with the Federal Reserve’s interest rate decisions, which in 2025 have left policy rates unchanged. The president, along with his fellow real estate developers, prefers low interest rates. But the Federal Reserve Act protects the Fed chair’s removal except "for cause." The president may have cause in connection with building renovations at the Fed.

Chair Jerome Powell’s removal would enable the president to appoint a successor, most likely someone inclined to lower the Fed’s policy rate, called the Federal Funds rate or Fed Funds. Long-term interest rates, however, are set by financial markets and are sensitive to inflation expectations. And easier monetary policy would likely raise inflation expectations, raising bond and mortgage interest rates even as short-term interest rates fall.

President Trump’s animosity toward Powell has been public for a while. The law allows the president to replace the chair “for cause,” a phrase not defined in the act governing the Fed. The White House’s Office of Management and Budget director, Russell Vought, has sent Powell a challenging letter alleging mismanagement of the $2.5 billion building renovation. In common employment cases, courts generally put the burden of proof on the employer. However, the constitution grants the president authority to manage the executive branch as he sees fit, though subject to the law. At this point, it’s hard to know how the case would play out in a court of law.

Bond markets would most likely take a Powell firing as negative. Stock markets might like it, though that prediction is much more speculative.

Long-term bond interest rates reflect inflation expectations. The economist Irving Fisher described interest rates as the sum of the “real” interest rate plus expected inflation. Bond holders will be repaid in dollars (or whatever currency the bond is issued in). If the purchasing power of a dollar is falling, the interest rate must be higher to make bond holders whole. And the bond issuers will be willing to pay a higher interest rate, knowing that when the time comes to pay off the bond, they will be paying with dollars of lower purchasing power.

Common economic models predict that persistently lower interest rates will lead to more inflation. Lower rates would not be very inflationary in an economy with substantial slack, but currently unemployment is low and businesses have little excess capacity. With a new chair, short-term interest rates might fall - that’s the Fed’s mechanism for monetary policy - but the Fed cannot stop financial markets from repricing long-term interest rates.

Not only will Powell’s ouster raise long-term interest rates due to higher expected inflation, it may also diminish the value of American assets to international investors. The United States has been a large, relatively stable, low-inflation economy. Investors like that. But Powell’s ouster may also lead to expectations of more economic volatility: more booms and more busts.

The strongest argument for low inflation focuses on economic stability. Higher-inflation economies tend to have periodic anti-inflation monetary policies, which lead often to recessions, then followed by stimulative policies to get people employed again - which eventually lead to more inflation. Back in the late 1979-82 era, Fed chair Paul Volcker committed to bringing inflation down and keeping it down. There followed an amazingly long period with few recessions. Long-term interest rates gradually declined. The 10-year Treasury bond hit a high of 15% in the midst of the anti-inflation program in 1981. It dropped below 10% in the late 1980s, then kept falling. Before the pandemic, it had dropped below two percent. That was due to both expected inflation being low and economic stability expected to continue.

It's not baked in the cake that a Trump appointment of the Fed chair will immediately ease interest rates. Monetary policy is actually made by the Federal Open Market Committee. This group consists of the seven presidentially appointed governors (who serve long terms in office) plus four of the 12 regional Federal Reserve Bank presidents (who are appointed by their respective boards of directors). The other eight presidents participate in discussions but do not vote. The chair has power only to the extent that he or she can persuade the other committee members to go along. In the case of a push for lower interest rates coming from the White House, some committee members may disagree with the policy, and others may object to being pressured.

Yet, even if the FOMC does not do President Trump’s bidding, the global investment community may feel that White House pressure will eventually come. Thus, the prediction of higher long-term interest rates seems pretty certain if Trump dismisses Powell.

Stock prices present a greater forecasting challenge. Stock investors like lower short-term interest rates. That lowers current financing costs and is likely to provide short-term stimulus to the economy. But investors also dislike high long-term interest rates and economic volatility. Political chaos doesn't seem to be much of a detriment, at least on the evidence from the first six months of Trump’s second term of office.

Business leaders should probably stick to a base case for planning of continuation of current monetary policy. Those especially sensitive to long-term interest rates, such as real estate and construction, will want to develop bad-news contingency plans for higher interest rates. Some companies will benefit from lower short-term interest rates (corporate finance, auto sales). They need good-news contingency plans to take advantage of the possible cuts in short-term interest rates. And all companies should develop general contingency plans for a more volatile, boom/bust economy. Let’s hope that like insurance, those plans are never used.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Welltower And Realty Income: Time For A REIT Rotation
data.description:
Today's theme is sector rotation! Sector rotation is a strategy where investors move portfolio positions between different sectors based on the stage of the economic lifecycle. The idea is that some sectors tend to outperform while others underperform with a degree of regularity. By rotating their investments into underperforming sectors expected to rise and out of outperforming sectors expected to fall, you can spin a wheel of constant appreciation…in theory.

This strategy applies broadly across the market to a variety of asset classes. In fact, it's been a tried and true investment strategy for generations, proving to serve investors a fortuitous cycle. Over time, sector rotation has become less effective as transparent and market efficiency improve over years and years. Despite challenges, the strategy remains broadly successful, especially in niche asset classes, such as real estate.

It's no secret that REITs sit at the core of REITer's Digest. After all, it's the lion's share of my professional experience, so it's to be expected that REITs will come up frequently in my publications. Today will be no different as we explore sector rotation in commercial real estate.

Setting The Stage

Sector rotation as a broad strategy involves capitalizing on market exuberance and upswings as well as downturns. Asset classes go in and out of style in commercial real estate, but by and large, their staying power has remained through generations.

What do I mean?

Look at the big three: office, multifamily, and industrial. Each has had its peaks and valleys over long time horizons. Investors who were willing to sell at the peaks and confidently buy in the valleys have historically been rewarded. This strategy is effective. Even better, the trends are often relatively easy to identify.

In today's discussion, I will present an opportunity for sector rotation in real estate. We will take in incisive look into two of the top REITs in their respective sectors. While one company is currently trading at all-time highs, riding the momentum of key tailwinds, the other offers new value as renewed interest returns to net lease. I float the idea of selling one to buy the other based on valuation discrepancies in a thematic play as sectors begin to rotate.

Welltower (NYSE:WELL), the world's largest publicly traded senior living REIT, has surged over the past twelve months to all-time highs on account of market confidence and emerging demand drivers.

In contrast, Realty Income (NYSE:O), the blue chip of the net lease sector, remains in the doldrums following several punishing years that have dramatically impacted the company from the standpoint of operating challenges and opportunity cost.

While investors hold the status quo, I see emerging market dynamics that could suggest getting ahead of the game could be a wise decision.

The Sell: Welltower

Over the past several years, senior living demand has expanded significantly. The pandemic uncovered flaws and issues within senior living that cause market upheaval. Following several years of aging demographics and limited new deliveries coming online, there is once again a supply-demand mismatch in the marketplace. For WELL, the world's largest senior living REIT, this has materialized into revenue growth and increased demand.

According to Cushman & Wakefield, senior living fundamentals are strong and getting stronger.

  • U.S. senior living property market fundamentals continue to strengthen. Stabilized occupancy trended upward for the seventeenth consecutive quarter, surpassing 89% overall, with secondary markets reaching 90% occupancy, a level not obtained since 2017.
  • The number of occupied units reached a new all-time-high in Q1 2025 with net absorption outpacing supply growth by 2.5 to 1, as inventory growth remains near historic lows.
  • Annual rent growth, though tapered from prior quarters, has remained intact, averaging 3.9% in Q1 2025, a dip that is likely seasonal as the sector emerges from winter months. This outsized occupancy and rent growth has helped counterbalance short-term turbulence in the broader capital markets.

The market has taken note, and the company is currently trading at an extraordinary valuation from both an earnings multiple and share yield perspective. What was once a high yielding REIT is now considered a growth powerhouse by Mr. Market. Shares of WELL have more than doubled over the past three years as the company's earnings multiple balloon past 30x.

The question becomes if the company is able to deliver on the lofty expectations. Often in a slow-moving game like real estate, the answer is no.

In fact, it appears as though trouble is brewing in senior living. Just recently, one of WELL's tenants declared bankruptcy as mounting expenses in the industry overtook their revenue generating abilities. Historically, WELL has been able to navigate tenant issues including limiting ties with Genesis Healthcare ahead of their bankruptcy.

In 2021, Welltower announced it was cutting most of its ties with Genesis in a massive $880 million deal, which included terminating leases with the nursing home giant on 51 of its properties.

Even still, according to Bisnow, Genesis owes WELL a significant amount of money.

Genesis owes roughly $700M to secured creditors, including Welltower, a REIT with a $100B market cap, and Omega Healthcare Investors, another REIT it leases properties from, according to the WSJ.

Interestingly enough, this was identified as a key consideration in Hindenburg Research's short report on WELL published several years ago.

  • Red flags indicate that another of Welltower's distressed deals, a 2021 restructuring with its troubled 4th largest operator, Genesis Healthcare, mirrored the latest 'miracle' deal.
  • In late 2021, evidence suggests that Welltower quietly disposed of 21 of its distressed Genesis assets to a Gefner-affiliated firm, once again handing skilled nursing facilities over to an inexperienced manager and clearing its books of the mess.

The issue with WELL lies in the company's current valuation. While tailwinds continue to power the company ahead, trading at more than 30x FFO tells a cautionary tale for a senior living REIT which has historically been a high yielder. While WELL is priced to perfection, I am beginning to doubt the feasibility or likelihood that the market will deliver a senior living renaissance over the next couple of years. Considering shares of WELL are up around 50% over the past twelve months, taking profits looks appealing.

The question becomes where to put them.

The Buy: Realty Income

While O has established an impressive, generational track record, the company is facing a flat five year period. Shares appear to be stuck as the company faces increasing risks and a risk free rate that is challenging the value of the company's monthly dividend.

Shares have more or less been flat over the past seven years despite the company continuing to grow earnings per share and the dividend. While O's current dividend and AFFO yields are historically high, their attractiveness is limited when compared to a ten year treasury yielding 4.5%.

However, if rates are to decline, O presents a great opportunity. That said, the "if" in that sentence is powerful considering how long we have waited for some relief in rates. With that said, the tide appears to be turning.

Blackstone (BX) is making a considerable push into the net lease sector as long duration REITs become increasingly attractive. The massive alternative asset manager has been packing on net lease assets. Most surprisingly was the announcement that net lease real estate would become a material component of BX's mortgage REIT, the Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), as the fund struggles to redeploy repaid loans into a complicated commercial mortgage market. I suppose if you close your eyes and use your imagination, one could make the argument that net leased assets are a real estate credit investment.

"As we look ahead, we are leveraging the same Blackstone platform advantages and entrepreneurial DNA to look across the real estate credit universe and identify the best-suited incremental, strategic opportunities for our business," Katie Keenan, CEO of Blackstone Mortgage, said on the call. "With interest rates remaining elevated, a positive outlook for the U.S., consumer and essential needs-based retail showing resilient performance, we see a compelling setup today to build a credit-oriented, diversified net lease strategy."

The important takeaways here is that the smart money begins to move towards long leased assets, it could be a leading indicator that interest could soon return to net lease. For O, the world's largest net lease REIT and the de facto net lease index fund, this could become a thematic play.

While investors wait, the company's nearly 6% yield is ample to compensate us while we wait for the market to once again love the Big O. Better yet, investing today means buying in at 13.3x forward adjusted funds from operations per share. This is a far more reasonable earnings multiple than the >20x AFFO where O was priced several years ago. For a company with a solid balance sheet and a continued track record of dividend growth, this multiple is considerably more attractive and leaves plenty of room for future growth.

O does not come without certain risks and challenges. Over the past year, I've published several critical articles of O mainly discussing their issue with Mission Creep, driving their attention away from their bread and butter business.

My concern around O lies towards the future. As we illustrated, the company's growth has slowed over the past decade. Traditional growth drivers like acquisitions are no longer moving the needle as a percentage of enterprise value. This means changing share level metrics will also become more difficult as larger transactions become necessary.

Since I published that article in July, Realty Income has continued to expand and adapt their business including the addition of a private capital platform to generate asset management fees. While I see downsides, I must acknowledge their creativity and ingenuity. In the meantime, the longest running dividend in net lease continues to grow.

Key Risks

As with any investment strategy, there are risks that need to be considered. In this case, there are two categories of risk that should be assessed: strategic risks and opportunity cost.

In the first case, sector rotation carries key risks. The strategy involves timing the market by identifying low points and high points, which means trying to predict future trends. While using valuations as a guiding tool for identifying bubble-like situations across the market is helpful, it is not perfect. For example, people have been calling out tech as being due for a sector rotation for the past decade. Instead, the largest names have bucked the trend by delivering on their stratospheric growth estimates. Specific to the aforementioned trade, WELL could continue to deliver on peaking growth estimates and similarly, O could fail to make a timely turnaround. This possibility leads to the next risk which is opportunity cost.

Assuming the prediction of a slowdown in senior living and revival of net lease are correct, there are additional risk factors. For example, switching between two specific names, such as WELL and O, presents opportunity cost risk as other sectors or net lease REITs could outperform O. Going one step further, trading into a specific net lease REIT also introduces a layer of risk at a company level. As I have outlined before, O's business is quickly changing leaving a degree of execution risk. This could be mitigated by opting for an ETF targeting the net lease sector, like the Fundamental Income Net Lease Real Estate ETF (NETL).

Either risk would diminish the effectiveness of the proposed swap, even if the thematic predictions around each industry are correct. However, instead of focusing on making the perfect trade, there is value in identifying two sectors that are ready to rotate.

Key Takeaways

This article was intended to highlight two REITs that are of similar quality, but currently sitting on two ends of the sentiment spectrum. While senior living demand skyrockets, driving the multiple of WELL to an extreme level, net lease remains largely in the penalty box. However, eyes are beginning to turn towards assets with long leases and creditworthy tenants. Sitting in that chair, I see a ripe opportunity for sector rotation between two of the largest real estate companies. Trading WELL for O means a considerable change in valuation and dividend cash flow to the shareholder.

As illustrated above, shares of O are yielding more than twice shares of WELL, while enjoying a similar credit rating. In fact, I would argue that O's balance sheet is more conservative and likely better positioned for the next three years than the expansive, growing WELL. I continue to emphasize the importance of valuation as a factor of shareholder return. In fact, I would call it the single most important piece of the puzzle. Time for a REIT rotation!

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies - June 2025 Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalization-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalization-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), OR Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%, therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were recalculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points. The second change is related to the equally weighted index that was expanded to 20 companies.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025, March 2025, April 2025, May 2025.

Wheaton Precious Metals remains the biggest precious metals R&S company by market capitalization. In June, it even exceeded the $40 billion level for the first time in its history. Second-placed Franco-Nevada lags significantly, with a market capitalization of less than $32 billion. No major changes occurred in the ranking, only Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) outgrew Orogen Royalties (OGNRF), and at the very bottom, Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) outgrew Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA). Therefore, Silver Crown ended June as the smallest of the companies, with a market capitalization of slightly less than $13 million.

In general, June was a good month for the precious metals R&S companies. Some of them recorded impressive double-digit gains. On the other hand, five of them, including Franco-Nevada and Royal Gold, ended in red numbers. The highest gains were recorded by Star Royalties. Its more than 30% share price growth was fueled by positive news related to its Copperstone gold stream, moreover, the share price needed some correction, following the steep two-months long bearish trend. More than 30% growth experienced also Elemental. It was fueled by the announcement that Tether Investments became a major shareholder, with an option to increase its stake in the company to more than 50%. On the other hand, the biggest loss (-10.73%) experienced shares of Vox Royalty (VOXR). However, it is hard to connect the decline with a specific news.

In June, silver outperformed gold significantly. As a result, the share price of the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) grew by 9.37%, while the share price of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) grew only by 0.41%. This helped the share price of the VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) to increase by 2.78% and 3.48% respectively. The precious metals R&S indices provided some mixed results. The Precious Metals R&S Index grew only by 0.67% due to the weak performance of Royal Gold and Franco-Nevada. On the other hand, the value of the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index, where the smaller companies have much stronger weight, increased by 6.76%.

The June News

The news flow was relatively weak in June. No major deals took place. However, some companies came up with encouraging portfolio updates.

Franco-Nevada (FNV) announced that following engagement with the Government of Panama's legal counsel, it has agreed to suspend the arbitration proceedings related to the Cobre Panama mine.

Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) announced some senior management changes. The company was also named among Corporate Knights' 50 Best Corporate Citizens in Canada.

OR Royalties (OR) announced a $13 million acquisition of a 100% silver stream on Orla Mining's (ORLA) South Railroad project. The feasibility for the project should be completed this year, the permits are expected by the summer of 2026, with production expected to commence before the end of 2027. OR will be entitled to purchase 100% of silver produced at the mine, at ongoing payments equal to 15% of the prevailing silver price. The company also reported new developments at several assets included in its portfolio.

On June 9, OR announced an amendment to its revolving credit facility. The original C$550 million credit facility with an uncommitted accordion of up to C$200 million was converted into a $650 million credit facility with an uncommitted accordion of $200 million. The facility will mature on May 30, 2029, and it will bear an interest of SOFR or CORPA plus 1.45-2.75% p.a.

Sandstorm Gold (SAND) provided some portfolio news. The main news is that First Majestic Silver (AG) made a significant gold-silver discovery at the Santa Elena mine. The new Santo Nino vein has been traced over 1 km along strike, and 400 meters down-dip. Sandstorm owns a 20% gold stream that entitles it to gold purchases at ongoing payments of $487/toz gold. At the same time, Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF) has expanded the drill program at the Fruta del Norte mine to 108,000 metres. Some of the recent drill results include 72.8 g/t gold over 7.95 meters, or 40.6 g/t gold over 13.9 meters. Sandstorm owns a 0.9% NSR royalty on Fruta del Norte. And some great drill results also arrived from Entree Resources' (OTCQB:ERLFF) Hugo North Extension which is a part of the Oyu Tolgoi mine. Entree drilled 260 meters grading 3.29% copper and 2.08 g/t gold. Sandstorm owns a 0.33% copper and 4.47% gold and silver stream on Hugo North Extension, with ongoing payments of $0.5/lb copper, $220/toz gold, and $5/toz silver.

The company also declared the quarterly dividend. It will pay C$0.02 ($0.015) per share on July 25. Shareholders of record as of July 15 will be entitled to this payment.

Metalla Royalty & Streaming (MTA) announced a $40 million revolving credit facility with a $35 million accordion provided by the Bank of Montreal and National Bank Financial. The facility bears an interest of SOFR plus 2.5-3.5% p.a. At the same time, Metalla fully repaid its old convertible loan facility ($13.1 million). The company also released its 2025 Asset Handbook.

EMX Royalty (EMX) sold its Nordic generative enterprise for $335,000 to First Nordic Metals Corporation. EMX will also be entitled to a 1% NSR royalty on any newly generated projects in Sweden and Finland during the next five years.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) announced the receipt of Writ of Summons and Statement of Claim related to its September 2023 purchase of the Red Hill royalty. The plaintiff claims that he had the right of first refusal, which was breached.

The company also provided a portfolio update. The most important news is that earthworks, including box-cut excavation, have started at the Sulphur Springs copper-zinc-lead-silver project. An updated feasibility study for this project is expected by the end of this year. Vox holds an A$2/tonne production royalty capped at A$3.7 million, and A$0.8/tonne uncapped production royalty over the Kangaroo Caves deposit, a part of the Sulphur Springs project. Another positive news is that the permitting of the Bowdens silver mine is progressing (Vox holds a 0.85% Gross Revenue Royalty over the property).

On June 23, Vox announced that the Horseshoe Lights project is being fast-tracked to production, which could start before the end of this year. Vox holds a 3% NSR royalty over Horseshoe Lights.

As of June 30, Vox Royalty was added to the Russell 2000 and Russell 3000 stock indices.

Sailfish Royalty (OTCQX:SROYF) announced that the U.S. Bureau of Land Management issued the Final Environmental Impact Statement for the Spring Valley gold mine. The Record of Decision is expected by August 11, and the mine construction may start immediately thereafter, with the first gold production in 2028. Sailfish holds a 3% NSR royalty that covers the majority of the proposed open pit, with the remainder of the property covered by a 1.5% and a 0.5% NSR royalty respectively.

Orogen Royalties (OGNRF) reported Q1 financial results. The company generated revenues of $1.4 million, operating cash flow of $0.8 million, and net income of $0.8 million. Unfortunately, Orogen's cash flows remain fully dependent on the Ermitano royalty. As of the end of Q1, Orogen held cash of $18.3 million, and no debt.

On June 27, Orogen announced that its shareholders approved the acquisition of Orogen by Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM), announced in April.

Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) noted that Tether Investments has acquired 78,421,780 shares of Elemental from La Mancha Investments, at a price of C$1.55 per share. Tether owns 37.8% of Elemental now. Tether also has an option to increase its stake to 51.8% by acquiring shares held by Alpha 1 SPV.

Empress Royalty (OTCQX:EMPYF) granted 1.4 million stock options to purchase 1.4 million shares at a price of $0.68, 1.75 million restricted share units, and 650,000 deferred share units, to certain directors, officers, employees, and consultants of the company.

Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) provided an update regarding the 4% gold stream from Minera Alamos' (OTCQX:MAIFF) Copperstone gold mine. An updated technical study is expected in Q4, and the mine should be fast-tracked to restart production next year.

Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) announced the acquisition of a 4.5% royalty on silver produced at Kuya Silver's (OTCQB:KUYAF) Bethania silver mine, for $3 million in cash and $2 million in units (each unit consists of 1 share and 0.5 warrants with a strike price of C$13 and 3-year maturity). Silver Crown should receive 4,500 toz silver per quarter over the first 4 quarters, 9,000 toz silver per quarter over the next 4 quarters, and 12,375 toz silver per quarter over the remainder of the mine life. After 475,000 toz silver is delivered, the royalty will be reduced to 1%. The royalty will provide immediate cash flow.

On June 19, Silver Crown announced the closing of the first tranche of its non-brokered offering of units, worth $2 million.

The July Outlook

The month of July began with gold moving sideways, however, the silver price experienced a breakout and seems to be primed for the psychological barrier of $40/toz. The precious metals R&S companies haven't experienced major moves by now. With the exception of Royal Gold, whose share price dipped by more than 10% following the announcement of the Sandstorm Gold acquisition. For now, it looks like the overall impression of July will be mixed. Unless the gold price decides to make a more significant move to the upside or to the downside. The silver price seems to be ignored. Even Silver Crown has moved only slightly up so far.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PEO: Energy And Materials Equities CEF (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the Adams Natural Resources Fund (NYSE:PEO) for almost two years now. Our last article was published with a 'Sell' rating, and we argued that investors should simply use ETF building blocks to achieve the same results as PEO, but with a lower expense ratio.

With inflation rearing its ugly head again, we are going to revisit PEO, its performance and analytics, and frame this CEF in today's macro environment.

Let's see first if there are any notable portfolio changes

Firstly let us look at the industry and sub-industry breakdowns in PEO:

  • Integrated Oil & Gas: 36.4%
  • E&P: 21.4%
  • Storage & Transportation: 10.9%
  • Refining: 6.2%
  • Equipment & Services: 5.9%
  • Chemicals: 12.7%
  • Metals & Mining: 2.8%
  • Containers & Packaging: 1.8%
  • Construction materials: 1.3%

Since our last review the Energy section of the portfolio expanded a bit, with Materials now down slightly to roughly 19%. The largest names in the portfolio are still quite similar, being the top names one can find in the Materials Select Sector ETF (XLB) and the Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLE).

The CEF therefore continues to be a blend of mainly energy names with a large materials sleeve. We grant the fact that the name has been around for a very long time, but there are a few new funds in the market that try to capture the same dynamic, while others are layering in new risk factors to optimize long term returns. Let us have a look.

Performance

Since we last covered this name, let us see how the CEF did versus a simple portfolio blend and some newcomers in the space:

We are using a total return figure here in order to incorporate the fund dividends and normalize returns across CEF and ETF asset classes. PEO has done well, with a 17% total return, a figure that shines when compared to a newcomer in the space, namely the FlexShares Morningstar Upstream Natural Resources Fund (GUNR). GUNR is up only +10% versus +17% for PEO during the same time-frame.

Let us now go back to that simple portfolio composed of 20% XLB and 80% XLE: that portfolio would have been up +26% in the same timeframe, thus consolidating our prior analysis. In our prior article we assigned the CEF a 'Sell' not on a net basis (i.e., expecting a negative return), but on a comparative basis with a simple XLE/XLB portfolio. Turns out that simple works better.

Premium/Discount to NAV

The CEF has historically had a large discount to NAV:

That figure has contracted severely since the beginning of 2024, bringing the CEF to a -9% discount to NAV, a figure which is the top of the range for this name. Can the CEF go even higher? Yes. Does it need a tangible driver? You bet. The fund needs an outperformance in energy names to continue to tighten its discount to NAV or some aggressive corporate actions in the form of share repurchases.

Distributions are covered

What we like about this name is the fact that it does not overdistribute:

The fund does not try to lure in investors via very high distribution rates funded by return of capital, but passes on what it makes. As an investor you are getting here the long term returns of energy and materials equities. With a fairly stable NAV over time and an inexistent leverage ratio (sub 1%), the name uses the CEF structure to distill capital gains into dividends.

Inflationary pressures have a new escape valve

While historically energy and materials have been good sectors to ride inflation, the new macro-world has seen the emergence of gold and bitcoin as escape valves for capital preservation. In the past three years we have seen a parabolic move in the last two asset classes:

With the emergence of these two assets we have also seen tweaks in respect to inflation hedging funds, and we recently put an article forward which covers the VanEck Real Assets ETF (RAAX), which contains energy, materials but also gold and gold miners.

There are various reasons investors buy a fund like PEO, but given its structure (i.e. no leverage), the name represents a structural means of extracting dividends from a portfolio of energy and materials equities. If you are considering PEO for inflation hedging, also keep in mind that gold and crypto have seen a massive bid as of late.

Why an upgrade?

With inflation stickier than expected, we are of the opinion PEO will continue to produce robust net positive returns, although smaller than a simple XLE/XLB portfolio. Net positive returns, however, cannot be ignored, and an outperformance in the underlying holdings might even see the discount narrow further. In our mind these are factors to upgrade the CEF to 'Hold' when compared to our last rating. While savvy investors can indeed build their own portfolio using cheap simple ETF building blocks, some retail investors prefer the one-stop-shop nature of PEO and are happy to pay the 0.65% expense ratio for this endeavor. We are not the ones to argue with this view, especially when the CEF is correctly set up for persistent inflationary pressures.

Conclusion

PEO is a closed end fund. The name invests in energy and materials equities, and represents a robust way to extract dividends from the underlying asset class. The CEF has a covered distribution and stable NAV. With a persistent inflation picture PEO is set to continue to deliver net positive returns, thus an upgrade to 'Hold', even if using ETF building blocks is cheaper.
